nativeRange.habitat: Small, clear, shady streams, creeks, and waterholes in semi-rainforest and grasslands; prefers well-vegetated areas with moderate to strong flow, sandy or gravelly substrate, and submerged logs and branches; may occupy partly stagnant waterholes with leaf-litter bottoms during the dry season

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and back (Java fern, vallisneria, water sprite)
open central swimming corridor — length is more important than volume
driftwood and submerged branches mimicking Australian stream biotope
moderate water flow (powerhead or spray bar)

diet.notes: Opportunistic omnivore in the wild, feeding on aquatic and terrestrial insects, micro-crustaceans, filamentous algae, and small invertebrates. In the aquarium accepts high-quality flake and micro-pellets readily; supplement regularly with frozen and live foods (daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworm, mosquito larvae) to bring out peak coloration. Vegetable matter — spirulina flakes, blanched greens — should form part of the diet.

avoidWith: dwarf shrimp (Cherry shrimp, Neocaridina — adult fish may prey on them)
very small nano fish that may be intimidated by active swimmers
slow-moving long-finned fish (fin damage risk from boisterous activity)
Banded Gourami (Colisa fasciata / Trichogaster fasciata) — unrelated family Osphronemidae, occasionally confused by name similarity

summary: One of the largest and most spectacularly coloured rainbowfish, M. trifasciata comes in numerous locality-specific colour forms — Goyder River, Habgood River, and others — making it prized by collectors. Hardy and active; best kept in a long tank with a shoal of 6+ to see males display their full colours.
careNotes:
The Banded Rainbowfish is one of the largest members of the family Melanotaeniidae, with mature males reaching 13–15 cm and developing a deep, laterally compressed body with vivid colouration. All wild populations share the species’ diagnostic dark mid-lateral band and bright red/yellow median fins, but the exact body colour — from electric blue overlaid on orange-yellow to more muted greenish or blue-silver tones — varies dramatically between river localities. Commercially available locality strains include the Goyder River form (Northern Territory; intense blue-and-orange colouration with red fins), the Habgood River form, and several other Arnhem Land and Cape York populations; experienced keepers and collectors value keeping locality strains pure and avoid mixing them. The species’ range runs from Melville Island (Northern Territory) east through Arnhem Land and across Cape York Peninsula to the Mossman River near Cairns.

A tank of at least 120 cm in footprint length (200 L) is recommended; length matters more than volume because these fish are active mid-water shoalers that dart back and forth and are best appreciated — and best coloured — in long tanks. Keep in groups of 6 or more; single specimens are shy and pallid. A mix of males and females encourages natural display behaviour: rival males swim side-by-side, extending their fins and intensifying their colours. Full adult colouration takes around 12 months to develop. The species is skittish and prone to jumping — a tight-fitting lid is essential. Water quality should be maintained carefully; neutral to slightly alkaline, moderately hard water reflects their native stream chemistry. Live or frozen foods fed regularly are the single most effective way to enhance colour. Note: the Banded Gourami (Colisa fasciata or Trichogaster fasciata, family Osphronemidae) is an entirely unrelated fish from South Asia — confusion between the two names occurs occasionally among inexperienced hobbyists.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer that spawns into fine-leaved plants or spawning mops; not difficult to trigger in good condition. A separate breeding tank with slightly warmer water (26–28°C), fine-leaved vegetation or a Java moss mat, and a sponge filter is ideal. Males display intensified colouration and spar vigorously during courtship. Females deposit adhesive, golden eggs — typically 60–70 per spawning session, with multiple sessions possible over days. Eggs hatch in approximately 7–12 days depending on temperature. Parents will eat eggs and fry; remove adults or transfer the spawning medium after spawning is observed. Fry are small at hatching and require infusoria or commercial fry powder for the first week before accepting freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Raise water temperature gradually and provide ample live foods for best survival rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Melanotaenia trifasciata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided tank dimensions (120 × 30 × 30 cm / 110 L minimum), temperature (75–91°F / 24–33°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness (8–20°H), native range (Northern Territory and Queensland), habitat, diet, social requirements (shoal of 6–8+), breeding method (egg scatterer, 7–12 day hatch), locality colour variation, and skittishness/jumping notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Melanotaenia trifasciata
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13.0 cm SL), temperature (25–30°C), pH (7.0–8.0), dH (9–19), native range (Northern Territory and Queensland), taxonomy (Melanotaeniidae, Atheriniformes), habitat (streams and waterholes with rocky/gravel substrate, submerged vegetation), and conservation status (Least Concern).

additional.name: Fishes of Australia — Melanotaenia trifasciata
additional.notes: Provided detailed range (Melville Island, NT east to Mossman River, Cape York QLD), habitat description (small shady clear streams in semi-rainforest and grasslands, stagnant waterholes in dry season), diet (filamentous algae, aquatic and terrestrial insects, micro-crustaceans), and egg count (60–70 per spawning).

additional.name: Australian Museum — Banded Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (to 13 cm, commonly 8 cm), native range specifics (Blyth River NT, Dalhunty River Cape York QLD), morphology (compressed body, long-based dorsal and anal fins, dark lateral stripe, yellow/red median fins), multiple colour forms, and aquarium popularity.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Goyder River Banded Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Trade source confirming Goyder River locality as the most commercially prominent variant; tank size (55 gal / ~208 L for a school), temperature (75–91°F / 24–33°C), pH (6.5–8.0), kH (8–20), diet (high-quality dry foods + frozen/live supplements), peaceful temperament, group size (6+), and note that mature males are deeper-bodied and more intensely coloured.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer into fine-leaved plants or spawning mops; separate breeding tank recommended. Females deposit 60–70 adhesive golden eggs per session; eggs hatch in 7–12 days. Fry require infusoria or fry powder initially, then brine shrimp nauplii. Remove adults post-spawning.
